title: The Afternoon Leaf: A Stimulant, a Parliament, and a Ban
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain what khat is and why it sits at the edge of the season's questions.
Subject: Episode introduction | Run time: about 3 minutes

[WELCOME] Welcome back to Better Vice Club, Season 3, The Forbidden Leaf. This is Anthony McDonald. We have studied tobacco, cannabis, opioids, coca, and psychedelics. Today we reach the least known substance in the whole series, a leaf chewed every afternoon across the Horn of Africa and Yemen, called khat.

[HOOK] The honest note, as always. This is a history, geography, and policy class, never a how-to, and nothing here is encouragement to use anything. Khat is a mild stimulant, but it carries real health and dependence concerns, and we will be honest about them (World Health Organization, 2006). If you or someone you know is struggling with substance use, the SAMHSA helpline is free and confidential, 24 hours: 1-800-662-4357. Now the hook. In Yemen, Ethiopia, Somalia, and Kenya, people have chewed the fresh leaves of the khat plant for more than 600 years, not in a bar, but in a dedicated room, during long afternoon conversations where business is settled, disputes are mediated, and poetry is performed. To the communities who use it, khat is not a drug. It is closer to a parliament. And yet in 2014 the United Kingdom banned it, against the advice of its own scientists.

[TEACH] We read khat through four lenses. Geography asks where the plant grows and how its 48-hour shelf life shapes a whole air-freight economy. Social studies follows khat as a governance institution and tells the documented story of the British ban that ignored the evidence. Economics asks why this one crop escapes the corporate extraction that captured coffee, and what prohibition costs. And English language arts studies the poetry of the chewing room and the politics of how a leaf is described. Each lesson is short and ends with a quick question.

[REMEMBER] Hold this. Khat is the season's clearest example of the same plant being two completely different things depending on who is looking: a centuries-old governance and social institution to the communities who use it, and a foreign drug to be banned to the governments that criminalized it. Whose description wins is the whole question.

[CHECK] Quick check. To the communities who use it, what is khat closer to than a recreational drug? [Beat] A governance and social institution, closer to a parliament, where business and disputes are settled (Kennedy, 1987).

## Key Takeaways
- Khat is a mild stimulant leaf chewed across the Horn of Africa and Yemen for more than 600 years.
- This is education, not a how-to or an endorsement; khat carries real health and dependence concerns (World Health Organization, 2006). Help: SAMHSA 1-800-662-4357.
- To the communities who use it, khat is a governance and social institution, not a recreational drug.
- The United Kingdom banned khat in 2014 against the advice of its own scientific advisory council.

## Sources
- World Health Organization. (2006). Assessment of khat (Catha edulis Forsk). WHO Expert Committee on Drug Dependence.
- Kennedy, J. G. (1987). The flower of paradise: The institutionalized use of the drug qat in North Yemen. D. Reidel.

title: The 48-Hour Leaf
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain what khat needs to grow and why its short shelf life drives everything.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we opened the khat episode. Two quick questions.
One: to its communities, what is khat closer to than a drug? [Beat] A governance and social institution, like a parliament (Kennedy, 1987).
Two: what did the United Kingdom do in 2014? [Beat] Banned khat against the advice of its own scientists (World Health Organization, 2006).

[HOOK] Most crops in this series can be stored, shipped, and stockpiled. Khat cannot. Its power fades within about two days of being picked, and that single fact, the 48-hour clock, controls the entire trade.

[TEACH] The plant is Catha edulis, and it grows in the cool highlands, between about 1,500 and 2,500 meters, where the air stays mild even in tropical latitudes (Gebissa, 2004). It wants moderate rain of roughly 900 to 1,200 millimeters, well-drained volcanic soil, and steady warmth, conditions found in the Ethiopian highlands, the Yemeni mountain terraces, and Kenya's Meru Hills. Those three regions grow almost all the world's khat: Ethiopia around Harar, Kenya in Meru, and Yemen in its highland terraces.
Now the chemistry that runs the whole show. Khat's main active compound, cathinone, begins to break down within about 48 hours of harvest (World Health Organization, 2006). After that, it converts into a weaker compound, and the leaf is considered stale and culturally unacceptable. So khat cannot be dried, warehoused, or aged like coffee or tobacco. It has to go from the field to the chewer within two days, fresh. That single constraint, written into the plant's biology, shapes the farms, the roads, the airports, and the markets we will follow next. Khat is the freshest commodity in the entire series.

[PICTURE] Look at the map on this lesson. Picture three cool, green highland regions: Harar in Ethiopia, Meru in Kenya, the terraced mountains of Yemen. Now picture a clock starting the moment a leaf is picked at dawn, ticking down 48 hours. Everything that happens to that leaf, every truck, every flight, is a race against that clock.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: khat (Catha edulis) grows in the cool highlands of Ethiopia, Kenya, and Yemen, and its active compound, cathinone, degrades within about 48 hours of harvest, so the leaf cannot be stored and must reach the chewer fresh, a constraint that controls the entire trade (Gebissa, 2004; World Health Organization, 2006). The 48-hour clock runs everything.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why can khat not be warehoused like coffee? [Beat] Its active compound, cathinone, degrades within about 48 hours of harvest, so it must be consumed fresh (World Health Organization, 2006).

## Key Takeaways
- Khat (Catha edulis) grows in cool highlands at 1,500 to 2,500 meters, in Ethiopia, Kenya, and Yemen (Gebissa, 2004).
- Its main active compound, cathinone, degrades within about 48 hours of harvest (World Health Organization, 2006).
- After that it turns to a weaker compound, so stale khat is culturally unacceptable.
- Because it cannot be stored, khat must travel from field to chewer fresh, which drives the whole trade.

## Sources
- Gebissa, E. (2004). Leaf of Allah: Khat and agricultural transformation in Harerge, Ethiopia 1875-1991. Ohio University Press.
- World Health Organization. (2006). Assessment of khat (Catha edulis Forsk). WHO Expert Committee on Drug Dependence.

title: The Khat Express
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can trace khat's same-day supply chain and explain how freshness protects farmers.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we met the 48-hour leaf. Two quick questions.
One: where does khat grow? [Beat] The cool highlands of Ethiopia, Kenya, and Yemen (Gebissa, 2004).
Two: why can it not be warehoused? [Beat] Cathinone degrades within about 48 hours (World Health Organization, 2006).

[HOOK] Because khat must be consumed within two days of harvest, it cannot move like a normal crop. It moves like a living thing being rushed to a hospital, and that frantic speed turns out to protect the farmers in a way nothing else in this series does.

[TEACH] Follow the chain hour by hour (Carrier, 2007). The leaf is picked at dawn, around 6 to 8 in the morning, at peak freshness. By 9 it is bundled and wrapped in banana leaves to hold in humidity. By late morning it is trucked over rural roads to an airport at Addis Ababa, Dire Dawa, or Nairobi, where road quality decides how much of the 48 hours remains. Then it is flown out the same day, on dedicated cargo flights, to Djibouti, to Yemen, and historically to London and Minneapolis, reaching diaspora communities the same day it was cut. The whole journey is a race against the clock in the plant.
Here is the part that matters most for economics. Because khat cannot be stored, it cannot be bought up and consolidated by giant commodity traders the way coffee is. There is no warehouse to fill, no futures market to corner, no global brand to build. The supply chain stays short and local, and Ethiopian and Kenyan farmers capture a much higher share of the final price than coffee farmers ever do (Carrier, 2007). The plant's own chemistry does what political campaigns usually cannot: it keeps the money close to the people who grow it. Freshness is the farmers' protection.

[PICTURE] Look at the map on this lesson. Picture a leaf picked at dawn in the Ethiopian highlands, trucked down a winding road, loaded onto an afternoon flight, and chewed that same evening in Djibouti or, once, in London. No warehouse appears anywhere on this map. The whole system exists to beat a 48-hour clock.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: khat moves on a same-day air-freight chain, harvested at dawn and flown to diaspora markets within 48 hours, and because it cannot be stored or consolidated by big traders, Ethiopian and Kenyan farmers keep a far higher share of the price than coffee farmers do (Carrier, 2007). Freshness protects the farmer.

[CHECK] Quick check. How does khat's short shelf life protect farmers economically? [Beat] It cannot be stored or consolidated by big commodity traders, so the supply chain stays short and farmers capture more of the final value (Carrier, 2007).

## Key Takeaways
- Khat is picked at dawn, wrapped to hold humidity, and flown out the same day to beat the 48-hour clock (Carrier, 2007).
- The trade depends on air freight, not warehouses, to reach diaspora markets in time.
- Because it cannot be stored, khat escapes the corporate consolidation that captured coffee.
- Ethiopian and Kenyan farmers therefore keep a much higher share of the final price than coffee farmers do.

## Sources
- Carrier, N. (2007). Kenyan khat: The social life of a stimulant. Brill.
- World Health Organization. (2006). Assessment of khat (Catha edulis Forsk). WHO Expert Committee on Drug Dependence.

title: Yemen's Thirsty Crop
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how khat and water scarcity collide in Yemen and why there is no easy fix.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 5 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we followed the khat express. Two quick questions.
One: how does freshness protect khat farmers? [Beat] It cannot be stored or consolidated by big traders, so farmers keep more of the price (Carrier, 2007).
Two: how does khat reach diaspora markets in time? [Beat] By same-day air freight, beating the 48-hour clock (Carrier, 2007).

[HOOK] In Yemen, the same plant that supports millions of families is also helping to drain the country dry. It is one of the hardest trade-offs in this whole series, and there is no clean answer.

[TEACH] Yemen is among the most water-stressed countries on Earth, and its capital, Sana'a, has been at risk of running out of groundwater (Ward, 2015). Khat is a thirsty crop, and it uses an estimated 40 percent of all of Yemen's agricultural water. So why do farmers keep growing it? Because it pays. Khat is the most reliable cash income a Yemeni farmer can earn, far more dependable than food crops, and that gap has only widened since the civil war began in 2015 collapsed formal employment, leaving khat as one of the few viable livelihoods for rural families.
Here is the structural conflict, and it is brutal. Families need two things at once: cash income, which khat provides, and food security, which non-khat crops provide. Khat wins economically while food security slips away, and the water table drops underneath both. Now notice the geographic equity question. It would be easy to say, just stop growing khat to save the water. But Yemen's water crisis comes from many causes, climate, governance, and war, not khat alone, and pulling khat away without giving farmers another way to earn a living would destroy millions of livelihoods without fixing the water (Ward, 2015). The honest answer is that you cannot solve the water problem without first solving the income problem.

[PICTURE] Picture a Yemeni hillside terrace, green with khat, and beneath it a water table dropping year by year. The farmer knows the water is running low. He also knows that khat is the only crop that reliably feeds his family. Both things are true, and that is the trap.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: in water-scarce Yemen, khat uses about 40 percent of agricultural water, yet farmers grow it because it is their most reliable cash income, so removing it without an economic alternative would destroy livelihoods without solving a water crisis that has many causes (Ward, 2015). The income problem comes before the water problem.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why do Yemeni farmers keep growing water-hungry khat despite the water crisis? [Beat] Khat is their most reliable cash income, far more dependable than food crops, especially since the war collapsed other employment (Ward, 2015).

## Key Takeaways
- Yemen is among the most water-stressed nations, and khat uses about 40 percent of its agricultural water (Ward, 2015).
- Farmers grow khat anyway because it is their most reliable cash income, unlike food crops.
- The 2015 civil war collapsed other employment, making khat one of the few viable livelihoods.
- Removing khat without an economic alternative would destroy livelihoods without solving a water crisis that has many causes.

## Sources
- Ward, C. (2015). The water crisis in Yemen: Managing extreme water scarcity in the Middle East. I.B. Tauris.
- Carrier, N. (2007). Kenyan khat: The social life of a stimulant. Brill.

title: The Mafraj
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how the Yemeni mafraj functions as a governance institution, not a bar.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we looked at Yemen's thirsty crop. Two quick questions.
One: why do Yemeni farmers keep growing water-hungry khat? [Beat] Because it is their most reliable cash income, far more dependable than food crops, especially since the war collapsed other employment (Ward, 2015).
Two: roughly what share of Yemen's agricultural water does khat use? [Beat] About 40 percent (Ward, 2015).

[HOOK] Imagine an institution that settles business deals, ends clan feuds, and builds political agreement, an institution as important to its community as a courthouse or a city council. Now imagine it appears on no organizational chart, has no official title, and meets every single afternoon in a room at the top of a house. That room is the mafraj, and to understand khat in Yemen, you have to understand that it is not a bar. It is closer to a parliament.

[TEACH] Picture the top floor of a traditional Yemeni house, a room with large windows on every side, looking out over the mountains and the neighborhood below (Kennedy, 1987). The word mafraj means, roughly, the room with a view. Every afternoon the men of the household and their invited guests gather here, khat is shared, and the chewing begins. Then the real business of the day starts.
Here is what scholarship documents happening in that room (Kennedy, 1987). Business contracts get negotiated and agreed. Conflicts between families and between clans get mediated. Political consensus gets built through long discussion. News gets exchanged, and poetry gets recited. A deal that could not be closed in a formal meeting might get worked out in a mafraj session. A clan dispute that formal mechanisms could not settle might find its resolution there. This is where the actual governance of community life takes place.
Now, why does khat fit this work so well? Because of what it does, and what it does not do. Cathinone, khat's active compound, produces mild stimulation, a sustained, alert sociability (Kennedy, 1987). That is well-matched to the kind of extended, careful conversation that governance needs. A mafraj session typically runs three to four hours. That is long enough for a complex negotiation to develop, and the ritual form of the session keeps people engaged across all that time.
And here is the part most outsiders get backwards. The mafraj is not a venue for intoxication. It is not a bar or a tavern. It is not a place where impaired judgment is acceptable or normal. The whole social architecture of the room requires clarity, not impairment. You cannot mediate a clan feud while drunk. Khat's mild, alertness-promoting effect is part of what makes it appropriate to this setting in the first place. The substance matches the job.
If this pattern sounds familiar, it should. In Season 1, Episode 6, we documented kava's role in Pacific Island governance, the same idea of a plant providing the social architecture for community decision-making. The mafraj is Yemen's version of that. Different plant, different ocean, same human invention: a structured space where careful conversation can do the work that formal institutions cannot always finish.

[PICTURE] Picture that high room at golden hour, latticed light falling across the floor, the mountains framed in every window. Men sit along the walls, talking slowly and carefully for hours. A contract is being settled in one corner. A dispute is cooling in another. No judge, no podium, no sign on the door. The governing happens here, in the light, in the talk, every afternoon.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the mafraj is a dedicated room in a Yemeni house where daily khat sessions serve as the community's real governance institution, handling business deals, conflict mediation, political consensus, news, and poetry, and it works because khat's mild stimulation supports the long, clear-headed conversation that governance needs, not the impairment of a bar (Kennedy, 1987). The mafraj requires clarity, not intoxication.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why is it wrong to picture the mafraj as a Yemeni bar? [Beat] Because its social architecture requires clarity, not impairment, the work of negotiating deals and mediating disputes needs alert, careful judgment, and khat's mild stimulation supports exactly that (Kennedy, 1987).

## Key Takeaways
- The mafraj is a dedicated chewing room that functions as Yemen's primary governance institution, appearing on no organizational chart (Kennedy, 1987).
- Documented functions include business negotiation, inter-clan conflict mediation, political consensus, news exchange, and poetry recitation (Kennedy, 1987).
- Khat fits because cathinone's mild stimulation supports sustained, alert sociability across sessions of three to four hours (Kennedy, 1987).
- The mafraj is not a bar; its work requires clarity, not impairment.
- The pattern parallels kava's governance role in Pacific Island communities from Season 1, Episode 6.

## Sources
- Kennedy, J. G. (1987). The flower of paradise: The institutionalized use of the drug qat in North Yemen. D. Reidel.
- Ward, C. (2015). The water crisis in Yemen: Managing extreme water scarcity in the Middle East. I.B. Tauris.

title: Many Names, One Leaf
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how khat is woven into several East African and Yemeni cultures.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we sat in the mafraj. Two quick questions.
One: what is the mafraj used for? [Beat] Business negotiation, conflict mediation between families and clans, political consensus, news exchange, and poetry recitation, the real governance of Yemeni community life (Kennedy, 1987).
Two: why does the mafraj require clarity, not impairment? [Beat] Because settling deals and mediating disputes needs alert, careful judgment, and khat's mild stimulation supports that rather than working against it (Kennedy, 1987).

[HOOK] One shrub. The same green leaves of the same plant. But travel across the Red Sea and around the Horn of Africa and that one leaf collects a different name in every community it touches. Qat. Chat. Qaad. Miraa. Same plant, four names, and in each place a slightly different role woven deep into how people live together. Today we follow that single leaf across cultures.

[TEACH] Start in Yemen. There the leaf is qat, chewed in the mafraj we just visited, where it supports business and community governance (Kennedy, 1987). We already know that story, so use it as our anchor and watch how the same plant shows up elsewhere.
Cross to Ethiopia, to the Harar region and the surrounding Oromia. Here the leaf is chat, also written tschat, and the relationship runs deep. Harar has more than 600 years of documented khat cultivation (Gebissa, 2004). It appears in everyday social gatherings, in coffeehouses, and notably in religious study, where the alertness it brings has long been tied to long hours of devotion. In Ethiopia, khat is part of the ordinary fabric of social and religious life, used for centuries.
Now go to Somalia. There the leaf is qaad, also called jaad, and it sits at the center of the afternoon. The qaad session is the setting for inter-clan negotiation, for community decision-making, and for the performance of oral poetry, which is one of the great Somali art forms. If the Yemeni mafraj is a parliament in a room, the Somali qaad session plays a closely parallel role, a structured space where parties who might otherwise clash can talk across a long afternoon.
Then turn south to Kenya, to the Meru people on the slopes of Mount Kenya. There the leaf is miraa (Carrier, 2007). In Meru, miraa is two things at once. It is a social practice, woven into community life. And it is an economic livelihood crop that supports a large supply chain of farmers, sorters, and transporters. For many Meru families, miraa is not just something you chew, it is how you make a living.
So hold all four together. Qat in Yemen, chat in Ethiopia, qaad in Somalia, miraa in Kenya. The point is not that these cultures are the same, they are not, and we are not ranking them. The point is the shape they share. In each place the same plant carries a different name and a slightly different role, but in none of them is it a casual recreational drug. It is integrated into social bonding, governance, and livelihood. One leaf, many names, and everywhere a thread running through the life of a community.

[PICTURE] Picture the leaf itself, then picture four hands reaching for it. A merchant in a Yemeni mafraj. A student in a Harar coffeehouse with a religious text open in front of him. A circle of elders in a Somali courtyard trading verses of poetry. A Meru farmer on a green hillside who grew it and will sell it tomorrow. Same plant in every hand. A different word for it on every tongue.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the same plant is qat in Yemen, chat or tschat in Ethiopia with over 600 years of documented cultivation, qaad or jaad in Somalia, and miraa in Kenya, and in each culture it is deeply integrated into social bonding, governance, or livelihood rather than being a casual recreational drug (Gebissa, 2004; Carrier, 2007). One leaf, many names, one social thread.

[CHECK] Quick check. Name one culture's word for khat and the role it plays there. [Beat] Several answers work. In Kenya it is miraa, both a social practice and an economic livelihood crop for Meru farmers (Carrier, 2007). In Ethiopia it is chat, used in social gatherings, coffeehouses, and religious study, with over 600 years of cultivation in Harar (Gebissa, 2004).

## Key Takeaways
- In Yemen the leaf is qat, chewed in the mafraj for business and governance (Kennedy, 1987).
- In Ethiopia it is chat or tschat, with over 600 years of documented cultivation in Harar, used in social gatherings, coffeehouses, and religious study (Gebissa, 2004).
- In Somalia it is qaad or jaad, central to afternoon gatherings, inter-clan negotiation, and oral poetry.
- In Kenya it is miraa, both a social practice and an economic livelihood crop for Meru farmers (Carrier, 2007).
- One plant carries different names and roles, but in each culture it is integrated into social bonding and governance, not casual recreation.

## Sources
- Gebissa, E. (2004). Leaf of Allah: Khat and agricultural transformation in Harerge, Ethiopia 1875-1991. Ohio University Press.
- Carrier, N. (2007). Kenyan khat: The social life of a stimulant. Brill.
- Kennedy, J. G. (1987). The flower of paradise: The institutionalized use of the drug qat in North Yemen. D. Reidel.

title: The Ban Against the Evidence
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can document how the UK banned khat in 2014 against its own scientific advice.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we followed one leaf across cultures. Two quick questions.
One: name one culture's word for khat and its role. [Beat] Several work. In Somalia it is qaad or jaad, central to afternoon gatherings, inter-clan negotiation, and oral poetry. In Yemen it is qat, chewed in the mafraj for business and governance (Kennedy, 1987).
Two: how long has Ethiopia cultivated khat? [Beat] More than 600 years of documented cultivation in the Harar region (Gebissa, 2004).

[HOOK] Here is a strange thing that actually happened. A government asked its own scientific experts whether it should ban a substance. The experts studied it carefully and said no, do not ban it. The government banned it anyway. This is not a hypothetical. This is the United Kingdom and khat, and it is one of the clearest cases you will ever see of policy made against the evidence the government itself commissioned.

[TEACH] Start with the evidence. In 2013 the UK's Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs, the ACMD, conducted a formal review of khat (Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs, 2013). This is the government's own expert body, set up specifically to advise on drug policy. After reviewing the harms, the ACMD recommended that khat NOT be banned. They found that its harms did not meet the threshold required for legal control, and that by comparable measures khat was less harmful than alcohol and tobacco, two substances that remain legal in the UK.
The ACMD was not the only voice. The Kenyan government objected through diplomatic channels, asking the UK not to ban khat because of the economic harm a ban would do to Kenyan farmers (Klein, 2014). And UK community organizations from the Somali and Ethiopian diaspora raised concerns about cultural practice and community wellbeing. So the picture going into the decision was clear: the government's own scientists said no, the affected producer country said no, and the affected communities said no.
The government banned khat anyway, in 2014. The stated reason was not that khat was especially dangerous. It was that the UK wanted to prevent itself from becoming a distribution hub, a place khat would pass through on its way to other countries where it was already illegal (Klein, 2014). The ban was justified less by khat's harm to people in Britain and more by Britain's position in an international supply chain.
Now follow what the ban actually produced, because the consequences are documented (Klein, 2014). The Kenyan miraa export market to the UK was lost overnight, taking a major income source from farmers who had no warning and no transition help. An underground market took the place of the legal trade, and as with most prohibitions, prices went up and quality went down. The cultural practice itself became criminal for diaspora communities, so people doing in Britain what their grandparents did at home now risked a criminal record. And here is the result that ties it together: there was no significant reduction in khat use among community members. The ban did not stop the chewing. It moved it underground, raised the cost, lowered the quality, and criminalized a tradition, all against the government's own expert advice.
That is what we mean by policy against evidence. The disagreement here ran against the science, against the producer country, against the affected communities, and against the outcome data afterward, all at once. The episode is a case study in how a drug decision can be driven by politics and international positioning rather than by the harm evidence in front of the decision-makers.

[PICTURE] Picture two stacks of paper on a minister's desk. One stack is the ACMD report, the diplomatic note from Kenya, and the letters from Somali and Ethiopian community groups, all saying do not ban this. The other stack is a single political argument about not being a distribution hub. The minister reaches past the tall stack and picks up the thin one. That gesture is the whole story.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: in 2013 the UK's own ACMD reviewed khat and recommended against a ban, finding it less harmful than alcohol and tobacco, yet the government banned it in 2014 anyway to avoid being a distribution hub, and the documented results were a lost Kenyan export market, an underground trade with higher prices and lower quality, a criminalized cultural practice, and no real drop in use (Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs, 2013; Klein, 2014). The evidence said no, the ban happened anyway.

[CHECK] Quick check. What did the ACMD recommend about khat, and what did the UK government do? [Beat] The ACMD recommended NOT banning khat, finding its harms below the threshold for control and lower than alcohol and tobacco; the government banned it anyway in 2014 (Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs, 2013; Klein, 2014).

## Key Takeaways
- In 2013 the UK's own ACMD reviewed khat and recommended against a ban, finding it less harmful than alcohol and tobacco by comparable measures (Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs, 2013).
- The Kenyan government objected diplomatically over economic harm, and UK Somali and Ethiopian groups raised cultural and wellbeing concerns (Klein, 2014).
- The government banned khat in 2014 anyway, citing a wish to avoid becoming a distribution hub (Klein, 2014).
- Documented consequences: the Kenyan export market lost overnight, an underground market with higher prices and lower quality, a criminalized diaspora practice, and no significant drop in use (Klein, 2014).
- This is a clear case of policy made against the government's own evidence.

## Sources
- Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs. (2013). Khat: A review of its potential harms to the individual and communities in the UK. ACMD.
- Klein, A. (2014). Khat and the city. Routledge.
