A primary source for the policy-against-evidence lesson.","url":"https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/khat-report-2013","source_url":"Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs"}]

title: The Second Crop
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can describe khat's economic weight in Ethiopia, Kenya, and Yemen.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we looked at the ban against the evidence. Two quick questions.
One: what did the UK's Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs recommend in 2013? [Beat] It recommended against a ban, finding that khat's harms did not meet the threshold for control (Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs, 2013).
Two: name one documented consequence of the 2014 ban. [Beat] The legal UK import industry was eliminated and the Kenyan miraa export market was devastated (Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs, 2013).

[HOOK] We talk about khat as a leaf people chew. But for millions of families across the Horn of Africa and Yemen, it is something else entirely. It is the paycheck. It is the second crop that keeps the lights on. And once you see its scale, you stop thinking of it as a fringe habit and start seeing it as a pillar holding up whole economies.

[TEACH] Start in Ethiopia. Khat is the country's second-largest export crop, right behind coffee. It earns roughly 350 to 500 million dollars a year in export revenue, and it employs about 15 percent of the working-age population in the main producing regions (Gebissa, 2004). Picture that. In the areas where khat grows, nearly one in six working adults makes a living from it. In a country where formal wages are low, that income is not pocket money. It is rent, school fees, and food on the table for millions of families.
Now move south to Kenya. There the leaf is called miraa, grown on the slopes of Mount Kenya in Meru County. Miraa supports the livelihoods of about 500,000 people across its supply chain, from the farmers to the bundlers to the drivers who race it to the airport (Carrier, 2007). Before the 2014 UK ban, Kenya exported around 15 million pounds worth of miraa a year to the United Kingdom alone (Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs, 2013). That was a steady, reliable market, and half a million livelihoods leaned on it.
Then cross to Yemen, and the numbers climb higher still. There khat is not just one crop among many. It is about 10 to 15 percent of the entire national economy, and it employs roughly one in seven Yemeni workers (Ward, 2015). Think about that share. One slice of a country's whole output, and a whole slice of its working people, all tied to a single plant.
Here is the point to hold onto. Khat is not a side hobby for a few chewers. It is a central pillar of three national economies, the kind of weight you would expect from a major commodity. It belongs in the same conversation we had last episode about legal coca in Bolivia, a traditional crop carrying a national economy, legal at home, banned abroad. Same scale, same story.

[PICTURE] Picture three countries on a map. In Ethiopia, color in one of every six working adults in the khat regions. In Kenya, draw a line from Mount Kenya out to half a million people who depend on miraa. In Yemen, shade in one slice of seven of every worker, and one slice of seven to ten of the whole economy. That shaded weight is the answer to anyone who calls khat a fringe crop.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: khat is a central economic pillar, not a fringe crop. Ethiopia earns roughly 350 to 500 million dollars a year and employs about 15 percent of working-age adults in producing regions (Gebissa, 2004); Kenya's miraa supports about 500,000 livelihoods (Carrier, 2007); and Yemen depends on khat for about 10 to 15 percent of its whole economy (Ward, 2015). Comparable in scale to legal coca's role in Bolivia.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why is it wrong to call khat a fringe crop? [Beat] Because it carries the economic weight of a major commodity: hundreds of millions of dollars in Ethiopian exports, half a million Kenyan livelihoods, and 10 to 15 percent of Yemen's whole economy (Gebissa, 2004; Carrier, 2007; Ward, 2015).

## Key Takeaways
- Khat is Ethiopia's second-largest export crop, earning roughly 350 to 500 million dollars a year and employing about 15 percent of working-age adults in producing regions (Gebissa, 2004).
- Kenya's miraa supports about 500,000 livelihoods, and exported around 15 million pounds a year to the UK before the 2014 ban (Carrier, 2007; Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs, 2013).
- In Yemen, khat is about 10 to 15 percent of the whole economy and employs roughly one in seven workers (Ward, 2015).
- Khat is a central pillar of these economies, comparable in scale to legal coca's role in Bolivia.

## Sources
- Gebissa, E. (2004). Leaf of Allah: Khat and agricultural transformation in Harerge, Ethiopia 1875-1991. Ohio University Press.
- Carrier, N. (2007). Kenyan khat: The social life of a stimulant. Brill.
- Ward, C. (2015). The water crisis in Yemen: Managing extreme water scarcity in the Middle East. I.B. Tauris.
- Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs. (2013). Khat: A review of its potential harms to the individual and communities in the UK. ACMD.

title: Freshness as Protection
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain why khat's chemistry keeps more value with farmers than coffee's economics do.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we sized up the second crop. Two quick questions.
One: roughly how much does Ethiopia earn from khat exports each year? [Beat] About 350 to 500 million dollars a year (Gebissa, 2004).
Two: about what share of Yemen's whole economy is khat? [Beat] Roughly 10 to 15 percent (Ward, 2015).

[HOOK] In Season 1 we watched coffee farmers grow the world's favorite drink and keep almost none of the money it earns. Khat tells the opposite story. The farmer who grows it keeps a far bigger slice. And the strange part is that nothing political made it so. The leaf's own chemistry did it.

[TEACH] Line the two crops up side by side. Coffee, once it is roasted, keeps for months, even years. That long shelf life lets big companies buy it, store it, ship it, and brand it. So coffee is dominated by major multinationals. Its price is not set on the farm. It is set far away by futures markets in New York and London, where traders buy and sell paper claims on beans they will never touch. By the time you pay for a cup, the farmer who grew it has captured only about 2 to 6 percent of that retail price (Carrier, 2007). Almost all of the value is added and kept by everyone who comes after the farm: the trader, the roaster, the brand, the cafe.
Now look at khat. The leaf lasts only about 48 hours before its active compound breaks down. That one fact changes everything. You cannot warehouse khat. You cannot corner it on a futures market. You cannot build a global brand around a leaf that has to be chewed within two days of harvest. So there is almost no corporate layer at all. The price is set by regional demand and by diaspora communities who want fresh leaf, not by traders in a distant exchange. And because the chain stays short and local, the producer keeps a much bigger share of the final price, with estimates running from 15 to 30 percent or more in diaspora markets (Carrier, 2007). That is several times what a coffee farmer keeps.
Here is the key idea, and it is a quiet one. Khat's 48-hour shelf life acts as an involuntary protection. The same chemistry that makes the leaf so hard to ship also shields it from the corporate extraction that captured coffee and chocolate. No one fought for this. No fair-trade campaign won it. No law required it. The plant simply spoils too fast to be accumulated, traded, stored, or branded, and so the money stays closer to the people who grow it. It is a rare case where geography and chemistry produced an economic outcome that political advocacy usually cannot reach.

[PICTURE] Picture two pipelines. In the coffee pipeline the farmer stands at the far end of a long row of warehouses, traders, roasters, and brand offices, and only a few coins of every dollar trickle back to him. In the khat pipeline there is no row at all, just the farmer, the truck, the plane, and the chewer, all racing a two-day clock. The short pipe is the whole protection.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: coffee keeps for months, is run by multinationals, is priced on New York and London futures markets, and leaves the farmer with only about 2 to 6 percent of retail; khat lasts about 48 hours, has almost no corporate layer, is priced by regional and diaspora demand, cannot be warehoused or branded, and leaves the producer with 15 to 30 percent or more (Carrier, 2007). The 48-hour shelf life is an accidental shield.

[CHECK] Quick check. How does khat's 48-hour shelf life protect the farmer's share of the money? [Beat] Because the leaf spoils too fast to be warehoused, traded on futures markets, or branded, there is almost no corporate layer to capture value, so the producer keeps a much higher share than a coffee farmer does (Carrier, 2007).

## Key Takeaways
- Coffee keeps for months, is dominated by multinationals, is priced on New York and London futures markets, and leaves the farmer only about 2 to 6 percent of retail (Carrier, 2007).
- Khat lasts about 48 hours, has almost no corporate layer, and is priced by regional and diaspora demand rather than futures markets (Carrier, 2007).
- Because khat cannot be warehoused or branded, the producer keeps a much higher share, with estimates of 15 to 30 percent or more in diaspora markets (Carrier, 2007).
- Khat's 48-hour shelf life is an involuntary protection against the corporate extraction that captured coffee and chocolate.

## Sources
- Carrier, N. (2007). Kenyan khat: The social life of a stimulant. Brill.

title: The Formula of Prohibition
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can use the UK khat ban to show the repeating economic formula of prohibition across Season 3.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we saw freshness as protection. Two quick questions.
One: how does the farmer's share of coffee compare to the farmer's share of khat? [Beat] Coffee farmers keep only about 2 to 6 percent of retail, while khat producers keep 15 to 30 percent or more (Carrier, 2007).
Two: why does khat escape corporate consolidation? [Beat] Because it spoils in about 48 hours, it cannot be warehoused, traded on futures markets, or branded (Carrier, 2007).

[HOOK] We have a running pattern in this season. Every time a substance gets banned, the same chain of events plays out, almost like a formula you could write on a board. The UK khat ban is the cleanest example we have, because it happened recently, in plain view, with the receipts. Let us read the formula off of it.

[TEACH] Start with the before picture. Before 2014, the United Kingdom had a legal khat import industry serving its East African diaspora communities (Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs, 2013). That meant legal jobs in import and retail. It meant tax revenue from a legal trade. And it meant a stable, reliable market for Kenyan farmers, who shipped around 15 million pounds worth of miraa a year to British buyers (Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs, 2013). The system was lawful at every step, from the Meru hillside to the corner shop in London.
Now the after picture. The 2014 ban eliminated that legal industry overnight. The legal jobs were gone. The tax revenue was gone. And in their place an underground market arose, where criminal networks captured the margin that lawful businesses used to earn. With no legal source and no freshness standards, prices rose and quality fell. Across the water, the Kenyan export market was devastated, with no warning and no transition help for the farmers who depended on it. Community members in the UK now faced criminal penalties for possessing a leaf their families had chewed for generations. And here is the part the ban was supposed to deliver: there was no documented significant reduction in use (Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs, 2013). People kept chewing. Only now it was a crime.
So write the formula. Legal market, then ban, then underground market, then the same use levels, then criminal records for community members, then no benefit to the producing country, then profits for criminal networks. Read it again and notice what it does not contain. It does not contain less use. It does not contain a safer product. It does not contain a single winner among the farmers, the chewers, or the public purse. The only parties that gain are the underground operators who inherit the market and, possibly, the makers of legal stimulants who lose a competitor.
This is the quiet thesis of the whole season. That formula has appeared in every Season 3 episode, with cannabis, with cocaine, with coca, and now with khat. Different leaf, different community, different decade, same chain of consequences. When you can predict the outcome that precisely, the policy is no longer an experiment. It is a known result.

[PICTURE] Picture a board with seven boxes drawn left to right. Legal market. Ban. Underground market. Same use. Criminal records. No producer benefit. Criminal profits. Now picture the same seven boxes from the cannabis episode, the cocaine episode, the coca episode. The labels never change. Only the name of the leaf at the top of the board changes.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the UK khat ban ran the full formula. A legal market with jobs, tax revenue, and stable Kenyan farm income became, after 2014, an underground market with criminal networks holding the margin, higher prices, lower quality, a devastated Kenyan export trade, criminal records for community members, and no documented drop in use (Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs, 2013). Legal market, ban, underground market, same use, criminal records, no producer benefit, criminal profits. The same formula in every Season 3 episode.

[CHECK] Quick check. What outcome did the UK ban fail to produce, even though that was its whole point? [Beat] A reduction in use. There was no documented significant drop in community khat use after the ban, while the legal market was replaced by an underground one (Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs, 2013).

## Key Takeaways
- Before 2014, the UK had a legal khat import industry with jobs, tax revenue, and a stable market for Kenyan farmers (Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs, 2013).
- After the 2014 ban, the legal industry vanished, criminal networks captured the margin, prices rose, quality fell, and the Kenyan export market was devastated (Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs, 2013).
- There was no documented significant reduction in use, but community members now faced criminal penalties.
- The repeating formula, legal market to ban to underground market to same use to criminal records to no producer benefit to criminal profits, has appeared in every Season 3 episode.

## Sources
- Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs. (2013). Khat: A review of its potential harms to the individual and communities in the UK. ACMD.

title: Poetry in the Chewing Room
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how oral poetry traditions depend on the khat session as their social home.
Subject: English Language Arts | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we worked through the formula of prohibition. Two quick questions.
One: what is the repeating prohibition formula? [Beat] A panel of experts evaluates the harms, recommends against a ban, and a politician overrides the recommendation anyway (ACMD, 2013).
Two: name one consequence of the UK ban. [Beat] Kenyan miraa farmers lost their largest export market overnight, about 15 million pounds in annual revenue (Carrier, 2007).

[HOOK] Here is a question you might never have asked. Where does a poem live before anyone writes it down? For some of the oldest poetry traditions on Earth, the answer is a room full of people chewing khat. Take the room away and the poem loses its home.

[TEACH] Start with Somalia, which has one of the world's most developed oral poetry traditions. This is not a folk hobby. It is a central institution of Somali cultural life. The gabay (gah-BYE) is a long narrative poem built in complex meter, performed at khat sessions and public assemblies, and it carries political events, historical memory, and moral argument (Andrzejewski & Lewis, 1964). The shorter geeraar (geh-RAHR) is a performance poem with strong rhythm and repetition. A gabayaa, a Somali poet, composes over time and performs in the afternoon gathering, and the audience responds, debates, and answers back in verse.
Now cross the water to Yemen. In the mafraj, the top-floor room with a view, poets compose the qasida (kah-SEE-dah), a classical Arabic ode that can run to dozens of verses (Caton, 1990). The session runs three or four hours, which gives the poet both the time to sustain a long form and an attentive community audience to perform it for.
Here is the key idea, and it should sound familiar. Think back to the Mazatec velada chants and the Pacific kava ceremonies from earlier episodes. In every one of those cases, the substance is not the content of the poetry or the chant. The substance is part of the social architecture. It is what gives the tradition the time, the gathering, and the structure it needs to be performed at all. The khat session does the same work here. It is the standing appointment, the regular room, the shared afternoon that the poem depends on.
And that leads to the consequence. When the substance is criminalized or the session is disrupted, the oral tradition loses its primary institutional home. The poem does not have a building of its own. It does not have a stage that the city protects. It has the chewing room. Close the chewing room and you have not just banned a leaf. You have pulled the floor out from under a literature.

[PICTURE] Picture the mafraj in the late afternoon. Latticed windows, the mountains beyond, a circle of listeners settled in for hours. One man begins a gabay he has been shaping for weeks. The others lean in, then push back with verses of their own. No notebook appears. No microphone. The poem exists only because these people gather in this room, on this schedule, around this practice. That gathering is the page.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: Somali gabay and geeraar and the Yemeni qasida are performed inside khat sessions, and like the Mazatec velada and Pacific kava ceremonies, the substance is not the content of the poetry, it is the social architecture that gives the tradition the time and structure to be performed (Andrzejewski & Lewis, 1964; Caton, 1990). Take away the session and the oral tradition loses its home.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why does criminalizing khat threaten an oral poetry tradition? [Beat] Because the khat session is the primary performing space, the social architecture that gives the poetry time and structure, so disrupting it removes the tradition's main institutional home (Andrzejewski & Lewis, 1964; Caton, 1990).

## Key Takeaways
- Somali gabay are long narrative poems in complex meter, performed at khat sessions, carrying political events, historical memory, and moral argument (Andrzejewski & Lewis, 1964).
- The Yemeni qasida is a classical Arabic ode performed in the mafraj over a session that runs hours (Caton, 1990).
- Like the Mazatec velada and Pacific kava ceremonies, the substance is social architecture, not the content of the poetry.
- When khat is criminalized, the oral tradition loses its primary institutional home.

## Sources
- Andrzejewski, B. W., & Lewis, I. M. (1964). Somali poetry: An introduction. Clarendon Press.
- Caton, S. C. (1990). Peaks of Yemen I summon: Poetry as cultural practice in a North Yemeni tribe. University of California Press.

title: The Rhetoric of Who
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can analyze how the framing of khat shapes which communities are seen as legitimate.
Subject: English Language Arts | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we sat in on poetry in the chewing room. Two quick questions.
One: what role does the substance play in the oral tradition? [Beat] It is the social architecture, the time and structure of the session, not the content of the poetry itself (Andrzejewski & Lewis, 1964).
Two: name one poetry form tied to khat sessions. [Beat] The Somali gabay, the shorter geeraar, or the Yemeni qasida (Caton, 1990).

[HOOK] Read these two sentences. "Khat, the drug linked to Somali pirates." And, "Khat, the leaf chewed in Yemen's parliament." Same plant. Two completely different worlds. The gap between those sentences is not about chemistry. It is about who is allowed to be the subject of the story.

[TEACH] This is framing analysis, and khat is a clean place to practice it. Drug-policy language often links a substance to a specific population, and that linkage does rhetorical work. It quietly answers the question of who is seen as the problem and who is seen as legitimate.
Line up the examples. A UK Home Office line tied khat to "Somali pirates," which associates a cultural practice with criminality. News framing called it "the drug of choice among East African migrants," which frames a cultural practice as a migration problem. Now read the other side. The ACMD used neutral phrasing, noting that khat use is associated with cultural traditions in East African communities, which centers cultural context (ACMD, 2013). Kennedy described the qat session as "Yemen's parliament," which centers governance (Kennedy, 1987). And scholarship on Somali poetry centers literature, framing khat as what supports the focused attention oral poetry requires.
Notice what is happening. Not one of these framings is technically false. Khat really does turn up in news stories about crime, and it really is the seat of Yemeni deliberation. The choice is which truth to put first.
That gives us the rhetorical principle. The question "What is khat?" cannot be answered without also answering "Khat for whom, and in what context?" So describing it in terms of migration and crime, rather than governance and literature, is not a neutral report. It is a political act. It shapes which communities are seen as legitimate participants in the debate and which are seen as a threat to be managed.
And that framing lands on real people. Klein describes the experience of legal alienation, the experience of having your cultural practice criminalized in your adopted country (Klein, 2014). When the public language about your practice centers crime and migration, you are written into the story as a suspect, not a citizen. That shapes how diaspora communities tell their own stories, often forcing them to argue for their belonging before they can even describe what the practice means to them.

[PICTURE] Picture two newspaper headlines pinned side by side on a wall. The left one says "Pirates' drug seized at border." The right one says "Inside the room where Yemen settles its disputes." Under both headlines is the same photograph: a circle of people, an afternoon, a handful of green leaves. The facts in the photo never changed. Only the sentence above it did, and that sentence decided who you were supposed to fear and who you were supposed to respect.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: drug-policy language links khat to a population, and that linkage is rhetorical, so framing it as crime and migration rather than governance and literature is itself a political act that shapes who is seen as legitimate (Kennedy, 1987; Klein, 2014). The experience of legal alienation, having your practice criminalized in your adopted country, shapes how diaspora communities get to tell their own stories.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why is the choice to call khat a "migrant drug" rather than "Yemen's parliament" a political act? [Beat] Because "What is khat?" cannot be answered without answering "for whom and in what context," so foregrounding crime and migration instead of governance and literature decides which communities look legitimate, which is a rhetorical and political choice, not a neutral one (Kennedy, 1987; Klein, 2014).

## Key Takeaways
- Drug-policy language links a substance to a population, and that linkage does rhetorical work, shaping who is seen as the problem.
- The same khat is framed as a "pirates' drug," a "migrant drug," a cultural tradition, "Yemen's parliament," or the home of a literature, and each framing centers a different truth (Kennedy, 1987).
- "What is khat?" cannot be answered without "for whom, and in what context?", so the framing choice is a political act.
- Legal alienation, the criminalizing of a cultural practice in an adopted country, shapes how diaspora communities tell their own stories (Klein, 2014).

## Sources
- Kennedy, J. G. (1987). The flower of paradise: The institutionalized use of the drug qat in North Yemen. D. Reidel.
- Klein, A. (2014). Khat and the city. Routledge.

title: Key Terms: Khat
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can define the core vocabulary from Episode 20 across all four lenses.
Subject: Vocabulary | Run time: about 5 minutes

Your word bank for the khat episode. Read it quick, out loud if you can. The full glossary lives in the course Resources. If you or someone you know is struggling with substance use, the SAMHSA helpline is 1-800-662-4357.

## Geography and Chemistry

**Khat** (kaht). A mild stimulant leaf (Catha edulis) chewed across the Horn of Africa and Yemen for centuries (Gebissa, 2004).

**Cathinone** (KATH-ih-nohn). Khat's main active compound, which degrades within about 48 hours of harvest (World Health Organization, 2006).

**Cathine** (KATH-een). The weaker compound left after cathinone degrades, which is why stale khat is culturally unacceptable.

## Social Studies

**Mafraj** (MAF-rahj). The Yemeni chewing room, a governance and social institution for negotiation, mediation, and poetry (Kennedy, 1987).

**Qat, chat, qaad, miraa.** The names for the same leaf in Yemen, Ethiopia, Somalia, and Kenya (Gebissa, 2004; Carrier, 2007).

**ACMD.** The UK Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs, which recommended in 2013 not to ban khat (Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs, 2013).

**Evidence-based policy.** Policy derived from systematic evaluation of evidence, which the 2014 ban overruled.

## Economics

**Social architecture.** The institutional structures that organize community social life, such as the mafraj (Kennedy, 1987).

## English Language Arts

**Gabay** (GAH-bye). A long Somali narrative poem in complex meter, performed at khat sessions (Andrzejewski & Lewis, 1964).

**Qasida** (kah-SEE-dah). A classical Arabic ode performed in the Yemeni mafraj (Caton, 1990).

**Oral literature.** Literature transmitted orally within communities rather than written.

**Framing.** The choices about which aspects of an issue to emphasize, such as khat-as-crime versus khat-as-culture.

**Legal alienation.** The experience of having your cultural practice criminalized in your adopted country (Klein, 2014).

## Sources
- Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs. (2013). Khat: A review of its potential harms to the individual and communities in the UK. ACMD.
- Andrzejewski, B. W., & Lewis, I. M. (1964). Somali poetry: An introduction. Clarendon Press.
- Carrier, N. (2007). Kenyan khat: The social life of a stimulant. Brill.
- Caton, S. C. (1990). Peaks of Yemen I summon: Poetry as cultural practice in a North Yemeni tribe. University of California Press.
- Gebissa, E. (2004). Leaf of Allah: Khat and agricultural transformation in Harerge, Ethiopia 1875-1991. Ohio University Press.
- Kennedy, J. G. (1987). The flower of paradise: The institutionalized use of the drug qat in North Yemen. D. Reidel.
- Klein, A. (2014). Khat and the city. Routledge.
- World Health Organization. (2006). Assessment of khat (Catha edulis Forsk). WHO Expert Committee on Drug Dependence.

title: Cumulative Review: Khat
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can recall the top facts from all four lenses and see how they connect.
Subject: Review | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Let's pull the khat episode together, one quick fact per lens, then the thread that ties them.

[TEACH] Start with geography. Khat (Catha edulis) grows in the cool highlands of Ethiopia, Kenya, and Yemen, and its active compound, cathinone, degrades within about 48 hours of harvest (Gebissa, 2004; World Health Organization, 2006). That 48-hour clock forces a same-day air-freight supply chain, and because the leaf cannot be warehoused, it escapes corporate consolidation, so farmers keep more of the price (Carrier, 2007). In Yemen, though, khat uses about 40 percent of agricultural water, a hard trade-off with no clean answer (Ward, 2015).

Now social studies. The Yemeni mafraj is a governance institution, where business is negotiated and disputes mediated, and khat's mild effect suits the careful, sustained conversation that needs clarity, not impairment (Kennedy, 1987). The same leaf is woven into Yemeni, Ethiopian, Somali, and Kenyan life under different names. And in 2014 the United Kingdom banned khat against the advice of its own council, which had found it less harmful than alcohol and tobacco (Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs, 2013).

Then economics. Khat is a central pillar, roughly 350 to 500 million dollars a year in Ethiopia, half a million livelihoods in Kenya, and 10 to 15 percent of Yemen's GDP (Gebissa, 2004; Carrier, 2007; Ward, 2015). Its 48-hour shelf life keeps producers' share far above coffee's. And the UK ban followed the season's repeating formula: legal market to ban to underground market to the same use to criminal records to criminal profit.

Last, language. The Somali gabay and Yemeni qasida are oral literature performed in the khat session, where the leaf is not the content but the social architecture (Andrzejewski & Lewis, 1964; Caton, 1990). And the framing of khat, as crime or as governance, is a political act, since the question "What is khat?" depends on "khat for whom?" (Klein, 2014).

[REMEMBER] Here is the thread. Khat is the season's clearest example of one plant being two different things at once: a centuries-old governance and social institution to the communities who use it, and a foreign drug to be banned to the governments that criminalized it. Geography explains its strange economics, history explains its meaning, and language explains how a description becomes a policy. Whose description wins is the whole question.

[CHECK] One last check before the quiz. What two things is khat at the same time? [Beat] A governance and social institution to its communities, and a drug to be banned to the governments that criminalized it (Kennedy, 1987).

## Key Takeaways
- Geography: khat grows in cool highlands, degrades in 48 hours, and its freshness protects farmers, but it strains Yemen's water (Gebissa, 2004; Carrier, 2007; Ward, 2015).
- Social studies: the mafraj is a governance institution, and the UK banned khat in 2014 against its own evidence (Kennedy, 1987; Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs, 2013).
- Economics: khat is a central pillar of three economies, and the ban followed the season's prohibition formula.
- Language: the oral poetry depends on the khat session, and how khat is framed is itself a political act (Andrzejewski & Lewis, 1964; Klein, 2014).

## Sources
- Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs. (2013). Khat: A review of its potential harms. ACMD.
- Andrzejewski, B. W., & Lewis, I. M. (1964). Somali poetry: An introduction. Clarendon Press.
- Carrier, N. (2007). Kenyan khat: The social life of a stimulant. Brill.
- Caton, S. C. (1990). Peaks of Yemen I summon. University of California Press.
- Gebissa, E. (2004). Leaf of Allah. Ohio University Press.
- Kennedy, J. G. (1987). The flower of paradise. D. Reidel.
- Klein, A. (2014). Khat and the city. Routledge.
- Ward, C. (2015). The water crisis in Yemen. I.B. Tauris.
- World Health Organization. (2006). Assessment of khat (Catha edulis Forsk). WHO.

title: Sources and Further Reading: Khat
text_content:
Every claim in this episode is cited inline so you can check it yourself. Here is the full reference list in APA style. Use it to verify a fact, to dig deeper, or to start your own research for the project. The episode is a history, geography, and policy class, honest that khat is a mild stimulant with real health and dependence concerns, and not an endorsement of use. If you or someone you know is struggling with substance use, the SAMHSA national helpline is 1-800-662-4357.

## References

Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs. (2013). Khat: A review of its potential harms to the individual and communities in the UK. ACMD. https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/khat-report-2013

Anderson, D., Beckerleg, S., Hailu, D., & Klein, A. (2007). The khat controversy: Stimulating the debate on drugs. Berg.

Andrzejewski, B. W., & Lewis, I. M. (1964). Somali poetry: An introduction. Clarendon Press.

Carrier, N. (2007). Kenyan khat: The social life of a stimulant. Brill.

Caton, S. C. (1990). "Peaks of Yemen I summon": Poetry as cultural practice in a North Yemeni tribe. University of California Press.

Gebissa, E. (2004). Leaf of Allah: Khat and agricultural transformation in Harerge, Ethiopia 1875-1991. Ohio University Press.

Kennedy, J. G. (1987). The flower of paradise: The institutionalized use of the drug qat in North Yemen. D. Reidel.

Klein, A. (2014). Khat and the city: An anthropological study of khat use in Mogadishu and in the diaspora. Routledge.

Nutt, D. J., King, L. A., & Phillips, L. D. (2010). Drug harms in the UK: A multicriteria decision analysis. The Lancet, 376(9752), 1558-1565.

Ward, C. (2015). The water crisis in Yemen: Managing extreme water scarcity in the Middle East. I.B. Tauris.

World Bank. (2023). Ethiopia economic update. World Bank Group. https://www.worldbank.org

World Health Organization. (2006). Assessment of khat (Catha edulis Forsk). WHO Expert Committee on Drug Dependence. https://www.who.int

title: Knowledge Check: Khat
text_content:
This check pulls from all four lenses in Episode 20: geography, social studies, economics, and language.

Each time you take it, you get 10 questions drawn from a larger pool, and the answer choices are shuffled, so a retake is never the same test twice. You need 80 percent to pass, and you can retake it as many times as you like. The goal is understanding, not gatekeeping.

After each question you see an explanation and a citation. Read them. The reasons a wrong answer is wrong are the best study material here.

A reminder before you begin: this is a history, geography, and policy class, not a how-to. Khat is a mild stimulant, but it carries real health and dependence concerns, and this is not an endorsement of use. If you or someone you know is struggling with substance use, the SAMHSA helpline is free, confidential, and open 24 hours at 1-800-662-4357.

When you finish, take the Project assignment, where the bigger questions, who decides what a plant means and what a ban really costs, get the long-form treatment they deserve.
quiz_content: {"passingScore":80,"attemptsAllowed":-1,"questionsPerAttempt":10,"shuffleOptions":true,"questions":[{"id":"q1","questionText":"To the communities who use it, what is khat closer to than a recreational drug?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q1a","text":"A governance and social institution, closer to a parliament"},{"id":"q1b","text":"A breakfast cereal"},{"id":"q1c","text":"A children's game"},{"id":"q1d","text":"A type of money"}],"correctOptionId":"q1a","explanation":"In Yemen, Ethiopia, Somalia, and Kenya, khat is used in structured gatherings where business is settled and disputes are mediated, making it closer to a governance institution than a recreational drug.","citation":"Lesson: The Afternoon Leaf (intro)"},{"id":"q2","questionText":"Why can khat not be warehoused like coffee?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q2a","text":"Its active compound, cathinone, degrades within about 48 hours of harvest"},{"id":"q2b","text":"It is too heavy to store"},{"id":"q2c","text":"It is illegal to own a warehouse"},{"id":"q2d","text":"It never spoils"}],"correctOptionId":"q2a","explanation":"Khat's main active compound, cathinone, breaks down within about 48 hours of harvest, so the leaf must be consumed fresh and cannot be dried, stored, or aged.","citation":"Lesson: The 48-Hour Leaf"},{"id":"q3","questionText":"Where does khat grow?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q3a","text":"In the cool highlands of Ethiopia, Kenya, and Yemen, at 1,500 to 2,500 meters"},{"id":"q3b","text":"In lowland tropical rainforest"},{"id":"q3c","text":"In Arctic tundra"},{"id":"q3d","text":"Only in greenhouses"}],"correctOptionId":"q3a","explanation":"Khat (Catha edulis) needs the mild temperatures of cool highlands between about 1,500 and 2,500 meters, found in the Ethiopian highlands, Kenya's Meru Hills, and Yemen's mountain terraces.","citation":"Lesson: The 48-Hour Leaf"},{"id":"q4","questionText":"How does khat's short shelf life protect farmers economically?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q4a","text":"It cannot be stored or consolidated by big commodity traders, so farmers keep more of the final value"},{"id":"q4b","text":"It makes the leaf more expensive to grow"},{"id":"q4c","text":"It guarantees a government subsidy"},{"id":"q4d","text":"It has no economic effect"}],"correctOptionId":"q4a","explanation":"Because khat cannot be warehoused or traded on futures markets, the supply chain stays short and local, so Ethiopian and Kenyan farmers capture a far higher share of the price than coffee farmers do.","citation":"Lesson: The Khat Express"},{"id":"q5","questionText":"How does khat reach diaspora markets in time?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q5a","text":"By same-day air freight, harvested at dawn and flown out to beat the 48-hour clock"},{"id":"q5b","text":"By slow cargo ship over several weeks"},{"id":"q5c","text":"By being frozen for months"},{"id":"q5d","text":"It is never exported"}],"correctOptionId":"q5a","explanation":"Khat is picked at dawn, wrapped to hold humidity, trucked to an airport, and flown out the same day, because it must reach the consumer within 48 hours of harvest.","citation":"Lesson: The Khat Express"},{"id":"q6","questionText":"Why do Yemeni farmers keep growing water-hungry khat despite the water crisis?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q6a","text":"Khat is their most reliable cash income, far more dependable than food crops"},{"id":"q6b","text":"The government forces them to"},{"id":"q6c","text":"Khat uses no water"},{"id":"q6d","text":"There is no water crisis"}],"correctOptionId":"q6a","explanation":"Khat uses about 40 percent of Yemen's agricultural water, but it is the most reliable cash income a farmer can earn, especially after the 2015 war collapsed other employment.","citation":"Lesson: Yemen's Thirsty Crop"},{"id":"q7","questionText":"What share of Yemen's agricultural water does khat use?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q7a","text":"About 40 percent"},{"id":"q7b","text":"About 1 percent"},{"id":"q7c","text":"None"},{"id":"q7d","text":"About 99 percent"}],"correctOptionId":"q7a","explanation":"Khat is a thirsty crop that uses an estimated 40 percent of all of Yemen's agricultural water, in one of the world's most water-stressed countries.","citation":"Lesson: Yemen's Thirsty Crop"},{"id":"q8","questionText":"What is the mafraj?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q8a","text":"Yemen's dedicated chewing room, a governance institution for negotiation and mediation"},{"id":"q8b","text":"A type of khat plant"},{"id":"q8c","text":"A brand of coffee"},{"id":"q8d","text":"A UK law"}],"correctOptionId":"q8a","explanation":"The mafraj is the Yemeni chewing room where documented functions include business negotiation, conflict mediation, political consensus, news exchange, and poetry, a governance institution that appears on no organizational chart.","citation":"Lesson: The Mafraj"},{"id":"q9","questionText":"Why does the mafraj require clarity rather than impairment?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q9a","text":"Its social architecture supports careful negotiation, and khat's mild effect suits sustained, alert conversation"},{"id":"q9b","text":"Because alcohol is served there"},{"id":"q9c","text":"Because no one talks in it"},{"id":"q9d","text":"Because it is a sleeping room"}],"correctOptionId":"q9a","explanation":"The mafraj is not a bar; its governance functions need clear judgment, and cathinone's mild stimulation produces the sustained, alert sociability that extended, careful negotiation requires.","citation":"Lesson: The Mafraj"},{"id":"q10","questionText":"Which of these is a name for khat in a specific culture?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q10a","text":"Miraa in Kenya (Meru), qat in Yemen, chat in Ethiopia, qaad in Somalia"},{"id":"q10b","text":"Espresso in all four"},{"id":"q10c","text":"It has only one name everywhere"},{"id":"q10d","text":"It has no names"}],"correctOptionId":"q10a","explanation":"The same leaf carries different names across cultures: qat in Yemen, chat or tschat in Ethiopia, qaad or jaad in Somalia, and miraa in Kenya, each deeply woven into social life.","citation":"Lesson: Many Names, One Leaf"},{"id":"q11","questionText":"How long has khat been cultivated in Ethiopia's Harar region?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q11a","text":"More than 600 years of documented cultivation"},{"id":"q11b","text":"About 5 years"},{"id":"q11c","text":"It has never been grown there"},{"id":"q11d","text":"Since 2014"}],"correctOptionId":"q11a","explanation":"Harar and Oromia have more than 600 years of documented khat cultivation, where it is used in social gatherings, coffeehouses, and religious study.","citation":"Lesson: Many Names, One Leaf"},{"id":"q12","questionText":"What did the UK's Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs (ACMD) recommend about khat in 2013?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q12a","text":"Not to ban it, finding its harms below the threshold for control and less than alcohol and tobacco"},{"id":"q12b","text":"To ban it immediately"},{"id":"q12c","text":"To make it mandatory"},{"id":"q12d","text":"It gave no opinion"}],"correctOptionId":"q12a","explanation":"The ACMD reviewed khat in 2013 and recommended against a ban, finding its harms did not meet the threshold for control and that it was less harmful than alcohol and tobacco by comparable measures.","citation":"Lesson: The Ban Against the Evidence"},{"id":"q13","questionText":"What did the UK government do in 2014, and how did it relate to the ACMD's advice?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q13a","text":"It banned khat anyway, overruling its own advisory council"},{"id":"q13b","text":"It followed the advice and kept khat legal"},{"id":"q13c","text":"It funded khat farming"},{"id":"q13d","text":"It did nothing"}],"correctOptionId":"q13a","explanation":"Despite the ACMD recommendation, the government banned khat in 2014, a documented case of policy against the scientific evidence, citing a wish to prevent the UK becoming a distribution hub.","citation":"Lesson: The Ban Against the Evidence"},{"id":"q14","questionText":"What was a documented consequence of the 2014 UK khat ban?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q14a","text":"An underground market arose, the Kenyan export market was lost, and diaspora use did not significantly drop"},{"id":"q14b","text":"Khat use fell to zero overnight"},{"id":"q14c","text":"Farmers got richer"},{"id":"q14d","text":"Nothing changed at all"}],"correctOptionId":"q14a","explanation":"After the ban the legal industry vanished, an underground market formed with higher prices and lower quality, Kenyan miraa exports were devastated, and there was no significant reduction in community use.","citation":"Lesson: The Ban Against the Evidence"},{"id":"q15","questionText":"How important is khat to Ethiopia's economy?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q15a","text":"It earns roughly 350 to 500 million dollars a year and employs about 15 percent of the working-age population in producing regions"},{"id":"q15b","text":"It earns nothing"},{"id":"q15c","text":"It is banned in Ethiopia"},{"id":"q15d","text":"It employs no one"}],"correctOptionId":"q15a","explanation":"Khat is a central economic pillar in Ethiopia, earning roughly 350 to 500 million dollars a year in exports and employing about 15 percent of working-age people in producing regions.","citation":"Lesson: The Second Crop"},{"id":"q16","questionText":"About how many people's livelihoods does Kenya's miraa trade support?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q16a","text":"About 500,000 people"},{"id":"q16b","text":"About 10 people"},{"id":"q16c","text":"Nobody"},{"id":"q16d","text":"About 5 people"}],"correctOptionId":"q16a","explanation":"Kenya's miraa supports the livelihoods of about 500,000 people across its supply chain, and exported around 15 million pounds a year to the UK before the 2014 ban.","citation":"Lesson: The Second Crop"},{"id":"q17","questionText":"How does the farmer's share of khat compare to coffee?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q17a","text":"Khat producers keep 15 to 30 percent or more, while coffee farmers keep only about 2 to 6 percent of retail"},{"id":"q17b","text":"They are exactly the same"},{"id":"q17c","text":"Coffee farmers keep more"},{"id":"q17d","text":"Neither keeps anything"}],"correctOptionId":"q17a","explanation":"Khat's 48-hour shelf life blocks corporate consolidation, so producers keep 15 to 30 percent or more, well above the 2 to 6 percent coffee farmers receive of the retail price.","citation":"Lesson: Freshness as Protection"},{"id":"q18","questionText":"Why does khat escape the corporate extraction that captured coffee?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q18a","text":"Its 48-hour shelf life means it cannot be warehoused, traded on futures markets, or branded globally"},{"id":"q18b","text":"Because governments protect khat farmers"},{"id":"q18c","text":"Because khat is more valuable than gold"},{"id":"q18d","text":"Because no one wants it"}],"correctOptionId":"q18a","explanation":"Because khat lasts only about 48 hours, it cannot be accumulated, stored, traded on futures markets, or branded, so geography and chemistry produce an economic protection that political advocacy usually cannot.","citation":"Lesson: Freshness as Protection"},{"id":"q19","questionText":"What is the repeating economic formula of prohibition seen across Season 3?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q19a","text":"Legal market to ban to underground market to the same use levels to criminal records and criminal-network profits"},{"id":"q19b","text":"Ban to instant elimination of all use"},{"id":"q19c","text":"Legalization to higher prices for farmers"},{"id":"q19d","text":"Prohibition to free treatment for all"}],"correctOptionId":"q19a","explanation":"The UK khat ban followed the same formula seen across Season 3: a legal market is banned, an underground market replaces it, use stays roughly the same, community members get criminal records, and criminal networks capture the profit.","citation":"Lesson: The Formula of Prohibition"},{"id":"q20","questionText":"What happened to Kenyan miraa farmers when the UK banned khat?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q20a","text":"They lost a stable export market overnight, devastating their incomes"},{"id":"q20b","text":"Their incomes doubled"},{"id":"q20c","text":"Nothing happened to them"},{"id":"q20d","text":"They received UK subsidies"}],"correctOptionId":"q20a","explanation":"The ban eliminated the legal UK import market overnight, devastating Kenyan miraa exports and the farmers who depended on them, despite Kenya's diplomatic objection.","citation":"Lesson: The Formula of Prohibition"},{"id":"q21","questionText":"What is the Somali gabay?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q21a","text":"A long narrative poem in complex meter, performed at khat sessions and public assemblies"},{"id":"q21b","text":"A type of cooking pot"},{"id":"q21c","text":"A khat farm"},{"id":"q21d","text":"A UK court"}],"correctOptionId":"q21a","explanation":"The gabay is a long Somali narrative poem in complex meter, carrying political events, historical memory, and moral argument, performed at khat sessions and public assemblies.","citation":"Lesson: Poetry in the Chewing Room"},{"id":"q22","questionText":"In these oral traditions, what role does khat play?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q22a","text":"It is not the content of the poetry but part of the social architecture that lets the tradition be performed"},{"id":"q22b","text":"It is the only subject the poems describe"},{"id":"q22c","text":"It replaces the poet"},{"id":"q22d","text":"It has no role"}],"correctOptionId":"q22a","explanation":"Like the Mazatec velada chants and Pacific kava ceremonies, khat is not the subject of the poetry; it is part of the social architecture that gives the tradition the time and structure to be performed.","citation":"Lesson: Poetry in the Chewing Room"},{"id":"q23","questionText":"What happens to an oral tradition when its substance is criminalized?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q23a","text":"It loses its primary institutional home, where the tradition was performed"},{"id":"q23b","text":"It becomes more popular than ever"},{"id":"q23c","text":"It turns into written law"},{"id":"q23d","text":"Nothing happens to it"}],"correctOptionId":"q23a","explanation":"Because the poetry depends on the gathering for its performance, criminalizing or disrupting the substance removes the social setting and the tradition loses its main institutional home.","citation":"Lesson: Poetry in the Chewing Room"},{"id":"q24","questionText":"What is the rhetorical principle behind 'the rhetoric of who'?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q24a","text":"The question 'What is khat?' cannot be answered without also answering 'khat for whom, and in what context?'"},{"id":"q24b","text":"All descriptions of khat are identical"},{"id":"q24c","text":"Only scientists may describe khat"},{"id":"q24d","text":"Khat has no meaning"}],"correctOptionId":"q24a","explanation":"Every description of khat is a rhetorical choice, so the answer to 'What is khat?' depends on who is using it and in what context, and the framing chosen is itself a political act.","citation":"Lesson: The Rhetoric of Who"},{"id":"q25","questionText":"How does describing khat as 'the drug of choice among migrants' work rhetorically, compared to calling the qat session 'Yemen's parliament'?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q25a","text":"The first frames it as a migration or crime problem; the second centers its governance function"},{"id":"q25b","text":"Both mean exactly the same thing"},{"id":"q25c","text":"Neither framing matters"},{"id":"q25d","text":"Both center literature"}],"correctOptionId":"q25a","explanation":"Linking khat to migration and crime frames whole communities as a problem, while centering governance or literature frames them as legitimate, showing how framing shapes the policy debate.","citation":"Lesson: The Rhetoric of Who"},{"id":"q26","questionText":"What is 'legal alienation'?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q26a","text":"The experience of having your cultural practice criminalized in your adopted country"},{"id":"q26b","text":"A type of visa"},{"id":"q26c","text":"A khat-growing technique"},{"id":"q26d","text":"A poem form"}],"correctOptionId":"q26a","explanation":"Legal alienation is the experience diaspora communities face when a cultural practice they brought with them is criminalized in their new home, shaping how they tell their own stories.","citation":"Lesson: The Rhetoric of Who"},{"id":"q27","questionText":"What is the central idea of the khat episode?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q27a","text":"The same plant is a governance institution to its communities and a foreign drug to be banned to the governments that criminalized it"},{"id":"q27b","text":"Khat has no history"},{"id":"q27c","text":"Khat is only grown in Europe"},{"id":"q27d","text":"Khat is a modern invention"}],"correctOptionId":"q27a","explanation":"Khat is the season's clearest case of one plant being two different things depending on who looks: a centuries-old social and governance institution, or a drug to ban. Whose description wins is the whole question.","citation":"Lesson: The Afternoon Leaf (intro)"},{"id":"q28","questionText":"What is cathinone?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q28a","text":"Khat's main active compound, which degrades within about 48 hours and converts to a weaker compound"},{"id":"q28b","text":"A Kenyan city"},{"id":"q28c","text":"A Somali poem"},{"id":"q28d","text":"A water-storage method"}],"correctOptionId":"q28a","explanation":"Cathinone is the primary active compound in fresh khat. It degrades within about 48 hours into a weaker compound, which is why stale khat is culturally unacceptable and the trade depends on speed.","citation":"Lesson: The 48-Hour Leaf"},{"id":"q29","questionText":"How does khat's governance role compare to kava from Season 1?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q29a","text":"Both provide the social architecture for communal decision-making and have been restricted by Western nations"},{"id":"q29b","text":"They have nothing in common"},{"id":"q29c","text":"Kava is a fruit and khat is a fish"},{"id":"q29d","text":"Both are fully banned everywhere"}],"correctOptionId":"q29a","explanation":"Khat and kava are both culturally specific plants that supply the social architecture for communal governance, both characterized as drugs by outsiders, and both restricted by Western nations.","citation":"Lesson: The Mafraj"},{"id":"q30","questionText":"How important is khat to Yemen's economy?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q30a","text":"It is about 10 to 15 percent of GDP and employs roughly 1 in 7 workers"},{"id":"q30b","text":"It is banned and earns nothing"},{"id":"q30c","text":"It is less than 1 percent of the economy"},{"id":"q30d","text":"It employs no one"}],"correctOptionId":"q30a","explanation":"Khat is about 10 to 15 percent of Yemen's GDP and employs roughly 1 in 7 workers, which is part of why it persists despite the water it consumes.","citation":"Lesson: The Second Crop"},{"id":"q31","questionText":"What is the honest trade-off at the heart of Yemen's khat economy?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q31a","text":"Families need cash income (khat) and food security (other crops) at once, and removing khat without an alternative would destroy livelihoods"},{"id":"q31b","text":"There is no trade-off"},{"id":"q31c","text":"Khat solves the water crisis"},{"id":"q31d","text":"Food crops pay better than khat"}],"correctOptionId":"q31a","explanation":"Khat wins economically while food security and the water table decline, so the water problem cannot be solved without first solving the income problem that makes khat farmers' only reliable cash crop.","citation":"Lesson: Yemen's Thirsty Crop"},{"id":"q32","questionText":"What is the Yemeni qasida in this context?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q32a","text":"A classical Arabic ode performed in the mafraj khat session"},{"id":"q32b","text":"A khat export tax"},{"id":"q32c","text":"A type of airplane"},{"id":"q32d","text":"A water pump"}],"correctOptionId":"q32a","explanation":"The qasida is a classical Arabic ode performed in the mafraj, where the khat session provides the time and social structure for extended poetic performance.","citation":"Lesson: Poetry in the Chewing Room"},{"id":"q33","questionText":"What did the UK government give as its stated reason for banning khat?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q33a","text":"To prevent the UK from becoming a distribution hub to countries where khat was already illegal"},{"id":"q33b","text":"Because khat was more harmful than heroin"},{"id":"q33c","text":"Because farmers requested it"},{"id":"q33d","text":"Because khat was extinct"}],"correctOptionId":"q33a","explanation":"The government's stated reason was to stop the UK becoming a hub for distributing khat to countries where it was already banned, not a finding of high harm, which its own council had rejected.","citation":"Lesson: The Ban Against the Evidence"},{"id":"q34","questionText":"What does the khat trade show about geography and economic power?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q34a","text":"A plant's chemistry and altitude can produce an economic protection for farmers that political advocacy usually cannot"},{"id":"q34b","text":"Geography never affects economics"},{"id":"q34c","text":"Only laws decide farmer income"},{"id":"q34d","text":"Altitude has no effect on trade"}],"correctOptionId":"q34a","explanation":"Khat's highland origin and 48-hour chemistry keep the supply chain short and local, delivering farmers a higher share of value, an economic outcome geography produced that advocacy alone rarely achieves.","citation":"Lesson: Freshness as Protection"},{"id":"q35","questionText":"Is khat treated as harmless in this episode?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q35a","text":"No, it is presented honestly as a mild stimulant that still carries real health and dependence concerns"},{"id":"q35b","text":"Yes, it is called completely safe"},{"id":"q35c","text":"It is described as a vitamin"},{"id":"q35d","text":"It is said to cure disease"}],"correctOptionId":"q35a","explanation":"The episode respects khat's cultural role while being honest that it is a mild stimulant with real health and dependence concerns, documented by the World Health Organization.","citation":"Lesson: The Afternoon Leaf (intro)"},{"id":"q36","questionText":"If you or someone you know is struggling with substance use, what resource does this episode give?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q36a","text":"The SAMHSA helpline, free and confidential 24/7 at 1-800-662-4357"},{"id":"q36b","text":"No resources exist"},{"id":"q36c","text":"Only a khat farm"},{"id":"q36d","text":"A poetry hotline"}],"correctOptionId":"q36a","explanation":"Every Season 3 episode points to the SAMHSA helpline, 1-800-662-4357, free, confidential, and open 24 hours, since khat carries real health and dependence concerns.","citation":"Lesson: The Afternoon Leaf (intro)"}]}
